title: Arrays and Lists
summary: Store collections of data in arrays and understand their fixed-size nature

sections.title: Arrays as a Pill Organizer
sections.content: Imagine a weekly pill organizer with seven compartments labeled Monday through Sunday. Each compartment holds exactly one day's pills, and you access them by their position (day 0 = Monday, day 1 = Tuesday, etc.). Java arrays work the same way: they're fixed-size containers where each slot holds one value of the same type. The key rule is that once you create an array with 7 slots, it always has exactly 7 slots — you can't add an 8th one later. You access each position using an index starting at 0.

sections.title: Creating and Accessing an Array
sections.content: This code stores exam scores for 5 students in an array and calculates the average

sections.title: Index Out of Bounds
sections.content: If you have 5 exam scores in an array, the valid indexes are 0, 1, 2, 3, and 4. Trying to access index 5 is like asking for the 6th slice of a pizza that was cut into only 5 slices — it doesn't exist. Java protects you by throwing an ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException when this happens, which crashes your program. This is one of the most common errors beginners face. Always remember: an array of size N has valid indexes from 0 to N-1.

sections.title: Off-by-one errors when looping through arrays
sections.content: Most common mistake: writing for (int i = 0; i <= scores.length; i++) when it should be i < scores.length. The <= tries to access the index equal to the length, which is one past the last valid element. Fix: always use < (not <=) when comparing the loop counter to the array length. Wrong: for (int i = 0; i <= scores.length; i++). Correct: for (int i = 0; i < scores.length; i++)

sections.title: Your turn
sections.prompt: A small business records daily revenue for a week: $120, $85, $200, $150, $90, $175, $130. Store these in an array. Write a loop that finds and prints the highest revenue day and its index (day number starting from 0).
hints: What variable can track the highest value found so far?
How do you compare the current element against the current maximum?
What should you initialize your 'max' variable to?
